Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Consciousness Science

Nikolaas Tinbergen

1907 – 1988

Ethology and the biological analysis of purposive behavior.

The most defensible reconstruction is this: Tinbergen does not give us a finished “philosophy of mind” in the usual analytic or theological sense. He gives us something more indirect but very powerful: a biological grammar for explaining minded life. His central object is not “mind” as an inner substance, nor “consciousness” as a first-person datum, nor “soul” as a theological entity. His central object is organized behaviour in a living organism situated in its natural environment. From that object, a philosophy of mind can be extracted, but only if we keep a strict distinction between what Tinbergen explicitly says, what his method implies, and what a later philosopher or theologian may responsibly build from him.

My reconstructed formula is:

Tinbergen’s philosophy of mind is an anti-Cartesian, anti-vitalist, anti-reductive, organism-environment naturalism in which mental life is approached through the integrated study of behaviour, bodily organization, development, ecology, function, and evolutionary history.

It is not classical behaviourism, because Tinbergen does not reduce behaviour to surface stimulus-response chains and is interested in internal organization, motivation, development, and physiology. It is not introspective psychology, because he refuses to let subjective vocabulary function as a causal explanation. It is not eliminative materialism, because his method brackets subjective experience rather than proving that experience does not exist. It is not theology, but it has theological consequences, especially for any doctrine of the soul that wants to avoid treating the soul as a ghostly rival to bodily life.

Tinbergen, born in The Hague in 1907 and later affiliated with Oxford, shared the 1973 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine with Karl von Frisch and Konrad Lorenz for discoveries concerning the “organization and elicitation” of individual and social behaviour patterns. The Nobel summary explicitly places him among the founders of ethology and emphasizes his experiments with dummies, exaggerated egg markings, and key stimuli.

1. The basic caution: Tinbergen’s “soul” is mostly absent, but not irrelevant

Tinbergen did not, so far as the central primary texts show, write a systematic doctrine of the soul, consciousness, or mind. His great theoretical intervention was methodological: how should behaviour be studied biologically? His 1963 paper, “On aims and methods of Ethology,” even uses the phrase “soul-searching,” but in context this is disciplinary self-examination, not a metaphysical theory of the soul.

That absence matters. A rigorous report should not pretend that Tinbergen was secretly a philosopher of consciousness or a theologian. But it would be equally wrong to conclude that he is irrelevant to philosophy of mind. Tinbergen is one of the figures who most decisively altered the terms in which animal and human mindedness could be scientifically discussed. The question after Tinbergen is no longer simply, “What is the inner mental state?” It becomes: “What is this organism doing, in what environment, under what causal controls, with what developmental history, with what function, and with what evolutionary background?”

That shift is philosophically enormous.

2. Historical position: Tinbergen between vitalism and behaviourism

The official Nobel press release situates ethology as a way out of two earlier extremes. On one side were vitalist views of instinct as mysterious, wise, inexplicable force. On the other side were reflexological or behaviourist approaches that treated instinctive action as chains of reflexes or emphasized learning in a way that often neglected natural context. Ethology’s distinctive move was to study behaviour comparatively, biologically, and in relation to survival value, species differences, and natural selection.

This historical position is essential for the philosophy of mind. Tinbergen is not saying, “The animal is a machine without inwardness.” Nor is he saying, “The animal acts because of an occult instinct-soul.” He is saying that behaviour must be made available to biological explanation. The living creature is neither a ghost inhabiting a body nor a mere mechanical aggregate. It is an organized animal whose actions must be studied as part of its adaptive equipment.

This is why Tinbergen’s method is more subtle than crude behaviourism. His own Nobel biography notes that he became bewildered by American psychology after the war, later wrote The Study of Instinct, worked at Oxford, and wanted to bridge ethology with neurophysiology and apply ethological methods to human behaviour. Secondary historical commentary similarly stresses that Tinbergen’s approach reacted against psychology that concentrated too much on learning and laboratory control while losing touch with naturally occurring phenomena.

So the first philosophical conclusion is:

Mind is not to be explained by invoking mysterious inner forces, but neither is it to be erased into laboratory stimulus-response regularities. Minded behaviour belongs to the biology of the whole organism in a world.

3. The four questions as the architecture of a Tinbergian philosophy of mind

Tinbergen’s 1963 statement is the central text. He defines ethology as “the biological study of behaviour,” characterized by an observable phenomenon, namely behaviour or movement, and by biological method. He insists that the study of behaviour requires multiple questions: causation, survival value, evolution, and ontogeny. These are not rival explanations; they are complementary dimensions that must be integrated.

In philosophy of mind terms, this yields a powerful explanatory schema.

3.1 Causation: What produces this behaviour here and now?

The causal question asks about immediate mechanisms: sensory triggers, nervous system organization, hormones, bodily condition, motivational conflict, feedback, and environment. For Tinbergen, causal explanation cannot stop at a phrase like “the animal is angry” or “the animal wants to escape.” Those may be humanly intelligible descriptions, but they are not yet biological explanations.

This is where his anti-subjectivism enters. Tinbergen criticizes explanations that answer “What causes this behaviour?” by appeal to subjective experience, since subjective experience is, by definition, observable only by the subject. He gives the kind of explanation he rejects: “the animal attacks because it feels angry.”

The philosophical point is not that animals never feel anger. It is that “feels angry” cannot be the terminal scientific explanation unless it is connected to observable behaviour, physiological processes, eliciting conditions, development, and function. Tinbergen’s rule is methodological discipline, not necessarily an ontological denial of experience.

3.2 Ontogeny: How did this behavioural capacity develop?

Tinbergen’s ontogenetic question is central to his rejection of simplistic nature/nurture oppositions. He argues that development should study changes in the “behaviour machinery” over the life of the individual and that “innateness” is often only an explanation by elimination, not a positive account. He also warns that “innate” is not simply the opposite of “learned” or “environment-induced.”

This is crucial for mind. Tinbergen’s implicit view is not: the mind is either genetically programmed or socially constructed. Rather:

The mind is developmentally organized. It is built through the interaction of inherited capacities, bodily maturation, environmental inputs, learning, and social conditions.

That makes Tinbergen relevant to contemporary developmental psychology, attachment theory, embodied cognition, evolutionary psychology, and developmental psychopathology. It also makes him relevant to theology: if the soul is thought of as the living form of an embodied creature, then the soul is not a static inner object but something whose expression unfolds developmentally in bodily and social life.

3.3 Survival value: What is this behaviour good for?

Tinbergen repeatedly treats behaviour patterns as comparable to organs. Just as an organ has a role in the life of the organism, behaviour can be studied by asking what it contributes to survival, reproduction, communication, protection, feeding, mating, parenting, or social coordination.

This is perhaps the deepest philosophical move in Tinbergen. Behaviour is not mere motion. It is organized, purposive-looking activity that can be studied without invoking conscious purpose as its cause. A gull chick’s pecking response, a stickleback’s courtship, a threat display, or a parent-offspring signal can have a function even when the animal is not consciously representing that function.

That gives us a non-Cartesian account of intentionality. Behaviour can be about the world, directed toward objects, responsive to signs, and embedded in practical significance without needing to be explained first as explicit inner representation. Tinbergen’s animals are not inert bodies moved from outside. They are organisms whose perceptual and motor systems are selectively attuned to meaningful features of their world.

3.4 Evolution: What is the history of this behavioural capacity?

Tinbergen’s fourth dimension is phylogenetic and comparative. Behaviour is species-specific and can be studied comparatively just as bodily structures can be. By the end of the 1963 paper, Tinbergen says behaviour can and must be studied in fundamentally the same way as other biological phenomena and as part of an animal’s adaptive equipment.

For philosophy of mind, this means that mind is not an all-or-nothing human possession. Minded capacities have histories. Perception, social signaling, parental care, fear, aggression, play, imitation, learning, and perhaps forms of awareness must be investigated comparatively. Tinbergen does not thereby prove that human consciousness is just animal behaviour with extra complexity. But he destroys the assumption that mind begins only where human verbal self-report begins.

4. Tinbergen’s anti-subjectivism: not a denial of consciousness, but a refusal of lazy explanation

Tinbergen’s most important negative doctrine is his opposition to “subjectivism.” But this must be interpreted carefully.

He objects when scientists explain animal behaviour by projecting human subjective states into animals. “It attacks because it is angry,” “it plays because it enjoys play,” or “it learns because it understands” are, for Tinbergen, not adequate explanations. They may sometimes gesture toward real affective or cognitive processes, but without independent evidence they risk anthropomorphic circularity.

There are three possible readings of this anti-subjectivism.

The first is eliminativist: animals have no subjective states. This is too strong and not warranted by Tinbergen’s method.

The second is behaviourist: subjective states are scientifically meaningless. This is also too strong, because Tinbergen is interested in internal organization, motivation, conflict, neurophysiology, and development.

The third is methodological: subjective vocabulary must not be used as a substitute for biological explanation. This is the best reading.

The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy’s entry on animal consciousness notes that European ethology, including Lorenz and Tinbergen, distanced itself both from purposive, mentalistic animal psychology and from American psychology’s lack of ecological sensitivity; as a result, questions about consciousness were marginalized, though later cognitive ethology revived them. This is exactly the right frame: Tinbergen sidelines consciousness as an explanatory starting point, but he does not settle the metaphysics of consciousness.

A Tinbergian study of consciousness would therefore begin not with the question “Does this animal have a soul?” or even “What is it like to be this animal?” but with a disciplined sequence:

What behaviour makes us ask about consciousness here? What are the causal mechanisms? How does the capacity develop? What ecological or social role does it play? What is its evolutionary distribution? What evidence distinguishes conscious from non-conscious performance?

A modern consciousness researcher, Yoram Gutfreund, makes precisely this move by arguing that if consciousness is a biological property, Tinbergen’s four questions should be applicable to it: mechanism, ontogeny, adaptive value, and phylogeny. Gutfreund also stresses the difficulty: consciousness research remains entangled with the mind-body problem and strong evolutionary claims about consciousness can be premature without a better account of the relation between brain, behaviour, and experience.

So the Tinbergian position is sober but not dismissive:

Consciousness should be biologically investigated, but not smuggled in as an unexplained explanatory cause.

5. The mind as organism-environment organization

Tinbergen’s deepest positive contribution is an ecological conception of behaviour. The animal is not first a private mind and then secondarily a body interacting with the world. The animal is a bodily agent already embedded in a field of relevant stimuli, conspecifics, predators, offspring, food, nests, territories, and signals.

His famous experiments show that animals respond to specific sign stimuli and sometimes to exaggerated or artificial stimuli more strongly than to natural ones. The Nobel press release mentions his work with dummies, key stimuli, “supernormal” stimuli, and stickleback behaviour. Philosophically, this means perception is not a passive mirror of the world. It is action-oriented selectivity. The animal’s world is filtered through what matters to its form of life.

This is one reason Tinbergen is compatible with later ecological, embodied, and enactive approaches to mind. He does not develop those philosophies, but he anticipates their basic pressure against disembodied mentalism. The mind is not sealed inside the head. It is expressed in bodily orientation, selective attention, posture, movement, approach, avoidance, signaling, and responsiveness to environmental affordances.

A French-language ethological reception makes this point in its own way by presenting Tinbergen’s 1963 text as a kind of manifesto for ethology and emphasizing the relation between animal, environment, individual development, and evolutionary comparison. Spanish-language work by Gustavo Caponi similarly treats Tinbergen’s four questions as relevant for cognitive ethology, including the study of cognitive capacities and emotional dispositions.

That multilingual reception matters because it prevents an overly Anglo-American reading in which Tinbergen is reduced to “animal behaviour science.” In broader philosophical terms, he is a theorist of living action.

6. Tinbergen’s late body-mind thesis: psychosomatic unity

Tinbergen’s most explicit comments about mind-body unity occur in his Nobel lecture, “Ethology and Stress Diseases.” The lecture is complicated because it contains both philosophically important insights and scientifically problematic claims, especially about autism. The body-mind material must be extracted carefully.

The important positive claim is that Tinbergen rejects a rigid mind/body division. In discussing stress, posture, bodily “use,” and the Alexander Technique, he argues for two-way traffic between brain and body and says the rigid distinction between body and mind has limited use and can hinder medical science.

This is one of the strongest passages for constructing a Tinbergian philosophy of mind. It gives us an explicitly embodied model:

Mental life is not merely in the brain. Bodily posture, muscular habit, stress, breathing, social insecurity, and feedback from movement can shape mental life. Likewise, fear, stress, and insecurity can be expressed bodily. Mind and body are not two separate substances communicating across a metaphysical gap; they are two abstractions from the life of one organism.

Tinbergen’s examples are not all equally well supported by contemporary evidence, but the philosophical move is important. He shifts from “mind causes bodily symptoms” or “body causes mental symptoms” to an organismic loop: bodily use, neural expectation, feedback, stress, affect, and social environment form a dynamic system.

This is close to what later theorists call embodied cognition, enactivism, psychosomatic medicine, or ecological psychology. Tinbergen’s own language remains that of ethology and biology, but the conceptual structure is recognizably anti-dualist.

7. Autism, human mind, and the danger of overextension

Tinbergen’s work on autism must be treated with particular care. It is philosophically revealing, ethically serious, and scientifically outdated.

In his Nobel lecture, Tinbergen argued that ethological observation of non-verbal expression could illuminate autism, and he interpreted autistic behaviour through conflicts between fear/withdrawal and social or exploratory tendencies. He and Elisabeth Tinbergen also emphasized anxiety-reducing, socializing approaches and suggested that many autistic children might recover if treated as traumatized rather than as genetically or organically impaired.

That autism theory should not be adopted today. Current public-health and medical sources describe autism spectrum disorder as a neurodevelopmental condition with multiple genetic, biological, and environmental factors; they do not support the idea that autism is caused by bad parenting.

The right philosophical extraction is therefore narrow but important. Tinbergen’s autism work shows his conviction that human mental life is readable through posture, gaze, approach, avoidance, stress, environment, and relationship. That remains a philosophically significant ethological insight. But the concrete causal theory he attached to autism was wrong and risked blaming parents despite his own insistence that he did not intend to do so.

Marga Vicedo’s historical analysis is useful here. She argues that Tinbergen crossed from animal to human studies partly because of his concern with the “human predicament,” his relation to John Bowlby, and the appeal of ethological approaches to human behaviour; she also shows that his autism work became entangled with broader claims about modern life, disadaptation, and stress.

The philosophical lesson is double:

Tinbergen’s method can reveal the embodied and relational dimensions of human mental life. But when transferred to humans, it requires exceptional caution, because ethological observation can become overconfident moral diagnosis.

8. Human mind: continuity without flattening

Tinbergen’s framework supports continuity between human and non-human animals, but not crude identity. The Nobel press release itself notes that ethological findings apply to mammals, including humans, and mentions human fixed action patterns, infant smiling, mother-newborn behaviour, psychiatry, and psychosomatic medicine.

Still, Tinbergen’s better interpreters emphasize that he was more cautious than Lorenz in extrapolating from animals to humans. Donald Dewsbury’s commentary on Elizabeth Hall’s 1974 interview says Tinbergen tested generalizations by applying methods from nonhuman studies to human behaviour, and that his nature-nurture views were close to later consensus in emphasizing interaction and plasticity.

A Tinbergian philosophy of human mind therefore says:

Human beings are animals, but not merely animals in the reductive sense. Human mental life is continuous with animal behavioural organization, but transformed by language, culture, symbolic systems, moral normativity, institutions, and self-interpretation. Ethology can illuminate the bodily and social base of mind, but it cannot by itself exhaust the meaning of human personhood.

This is where theology becomes relevant rather than decorative. Theology is one of the discourses that asks whether human behaviour has meanings irreducible to survival value: worship, sin, grace, vocation, love, suffering, sacrifice, contemplation, holiness, and the imago Dei. A Tinbergian method can study the behavioural, developmental, and social conditions of such phenomena. It cannot decide their full metaphysical or theological truth.

9. Non-English reception: especially the Dutch philosophical worry

The Dutch reception is especially valuable because it sees a philosophical problem that can be less visible in purely Anglo scientific readings.

A 1976 Dutch article by H. Jans on “Niko Tinbergen over ethologie” notes Tinbergen’s anti-metaphysical tendency, his rejection of human uniqueness as scientifically meaningful in certain contexts, and his attempt to create a common language for animal and human behaviour. But Jans also raises a worry: can the “meaning” of human gestures really be reduced to survival value, function, or utility? Might human behaviour contain lived-experience values beyond species survival?

This is one of the most important criticisms for constructing a philosophy of mind from Tinbergen. Tinbergen’s ethology is strongest when it explains behaviour as adaptive, organized, embodied action. It is weakest if “meaning” is reduced entirely to biological utility.

For example, a human embrace may have biological functions: bonding, reassurance, affiliation, stress reduction, social coordination. But it may also carry meanings that are not captured by survival vocabulary: forgiveness, grief, blessing, erotic tenderness, reconciliation, covenant, or sacramental sign. A Tinbergian account can illuminate the embodied basis of such acts. It cannot automatically exhaust their lived, moral, or theological meaning.

The Dutch critique therefore gives us an important correction:

Tinbergen naturalizes behaviour, but a complete philosophy of mind must distinguish biological function from lived meaning and normative significance.

10. Soul: what Tinbergen rules out, and what he leaves open

Tinbergen does not give us a doctrine of the soul. But his work strongly constrains what a plausible doctrine of the soul may look like.

10.1 What Tinbergen rules out

First, he rules out “soul” as an explanatory shortcut. One cannot explain a behaviour by saying that an animal’s soul, instinct, will, or inward essence simply made it happen. That would be a return to the vitalist mystery-language that ethology helped overcome. The Nobel press release’s contrast between ethology and older vitalist accounts of instinct is relevant here.

Second, he rules out a Cartesian picture in which the real mind is hidden inside and the body is merely an external instrument. His late Nobel lecture’s insistence on body-mind unity directly resists that dualism.

Third, he rules out a purely introspective psychology of animals. Since animal subjective experience cannot be directly observed by the human scientist, subjective vocabulary must be disciplined by behavioural, physiological, developmental, and ecological evidence.

10.2 What Tinbergen leaves open

Tinbergen leaves open a hylomorphic or organismic doctrine of soul. In Aristotelian and many Christian philosophical traditions, “soul” need not mean a ghostly object inside the body. It can mean the form, life-principle, or organizing actuality of a living body. Read that way, Tinbergen is not the enemy of soul-language. He is an enemy of bad soul-language: soul as gap-filler, soul as anti-biological force, soul as private ghost replacing explanation.

A Tinbergian-compatible doctrine of soul would say:

The soul is not an extra causal lever competing with neural, bodily, developmental, and ecological causes. The soul is the living organization of the creature as a whole. In human beings, the soul is expressed through embodied action, perception, affect, language, relationship, memory, attention, worship, moral agency, and vulnerability. Biological explanation describes the modes of the soul’s embodied operation; it does not necessarily abolish the soul.

This is not Tinbergen’s own doctrine. It is a constructive theological interpretation constrained by Tinbergen’s biology.

10.3 Theological anthropology: Tinbergen against disembodied spirituality

Tinbergen is most theologically useful as an anti-Gnostic thinker. He forces any theology of mind or soul to take the body seriously: posture, stress, gaze, gesture, development, attachment, fear, environment, animal continuity, and natural history.

A Christian theologian, for example, could use Tinbergen to argue that the soul is not best imagined as a detachable mental substance but as the personal life of an embodied creature. A Jewish or Christian theological anthropology of creation could also find support in Tinbergen’s reverent naturalism: creaturely life is ordered, meaningful, responsive, and worthy of careful attention. But such a theology must add what Tinbergen’s science does not provide: normativity, divine relation, moral responsibility, sin, grace, vocation, and eschatological hope.

Tinbergen’s own language of “watching and wondering” is relevant here. In the Nobel lecture, he praises close observation and warns against confusing correlation with cause-effect or being seduced by the glamour of apparatus. That methodological humility has a quasi-contemplative quality, even though it is not itself theology.

11. Consciousness: a Tinbergian reconstruction

A Tinbergian philosophy of consciousness would not begin with human self-consciousness and then ask whether animals possess an inferior copy. It would begin with living action and ask what kinds of behavioural organization require what kinds of awareness.

Here is the reconstructed position.

First, consciousness is not scientifically basic in Tinbergen’s framework. Behaviour is basic because it is observable and biologically tractable.

Second, consciousness cannot be inferred merely by anthropomorphic projection. Saying “the animal feels shame,” “the bird is proud,” or “the fish is angry” is not enough. Tinbergen’s critique of subjectivist explanation blocks that move.

Third, consciousness also cannot be dismissed merely because it is difficult to observe. A properly Tinbergian approach would operationalize the question: What behavioural flexibility, attention, memory, pain-response, social learning, self-monitoring, or affective regulation is being explained?

Fourth, self-report is crucial in humans but not metaphysically magical. For Tinbergen, speech is also behaviour; it must be interpreted as part of the organism’s total action. Human verbal testimony about experience deserves special epistemic status, but it does not remove the need for causal, developmental, functional, and historical explanation.

Fifth, the hard problem remains. Tinbergen’s framework can organize evidence about consciousness, but it does not by itself explain why neural and behavioural organization should be accompanied by felt experience. Gutfreund’s discussion is helpful here because it explicitly connects Tinbergen’s questions to consciousness while warning that the evolution of consciousness cannot be settled without a better account of the mind-body problem.

So the Tinbergian answer to consciousness is neither “animals are conscious just like us” nor “animals are unconscious machines.” It is:

Consciousness is a biological question, but it must be approached through convergent evidence across mechanism, development, function, and evolutionary history.

12. Mind: the Tinbergian account in positive form

If we extract a philosophy of mind from Tinbergen, it has the following principles.

1. Mind is not a separate object hidden behind behaviour. Mind is disclosed in the organization of behaviour: perception, movement, affect, orientation, communication, attention, social response, and bodily regulation.

2. Behaviour is not mere movement. Behaviour is biologically organized action. It is comparable to an organ in that it has structure, function, development, and evolutionary history.

3. Mental explanation is plural-level. A complete explanation must include immediate causation, ontogeny, survival value, and phylogeny. No single level replaces the others.

4. Subjective vocabulary must be disciplined. Terms such as anger, fear, desire, intention, or consciousness may be useful, but they must not substitute for testable explanation.

5. The mind is embodied. Bodily posture, stress, movement, feedback, and physiological regulation are not merely external expressions of mind; they are part of the organismic system in which mind operates.

6. The mind is ecological. The organism’s world is not neutral physical space. It is a field of relevant cues, affordances, dangers, mates, offspring, signals, and social possibilities. Tinbergen’s work on key stimuli and supernormal stimuli makes this especially clear.

7. The mind is developmental. Capacities emerge through life history. “Innate” and “learned” are not adequate opposites.

8. The mind is evolutionary. Mental and behavioural capacities have phylogenetic histories. Human mindedness must be understood in continuity with animal life, while still allowing for distinctively human language, culture, normativity, and self-interpretation.

9. Human mental life is not exhausted by ethology. Tinbergen provides indispensable biological constraints, but not a full account of meaning, truth, morality, theology, or first-person phenomenology. The Dutch reception’s concern about lived meaning beyond survival value is therefore philosophically important.

10. A doctrine of soul can survive Tinbergen only by becoming more embodied, not less. Soul-language that competes with biological explanation is weakened by Tinbergen. Soul-language that names the integrated life of an embodied creature is strengthened.

13. Theological implications in more detail

Tinbergen’s relevance to theology is not that he makes theological claims. It is that he forces theology to clarify what it means by soul, person, nature, and creaturely life.

13.1 Against the ghost-soul

If the soul is treated as an invisible inner agent that produces behaviour whenever biological explanation runs out, Tinbergen is devastating. Ethology shows that highly organized behaviour can be studied through stimuli, bodily mechanisms, development, function, and evolutionary history. The “soul” cannot be used as a refuge from explanation.

13.2 For an embodied soul

If the soul is understood as the life of the whole embodied person or organism, Tinbergen is helpful. His work makes visible the living unity of perception, posture, movement, affect, environment, and social relation. In this sense, Tinbergen can be read as supporting a non-Cartesian theology of the soul.

13.3 For creaturely continuity

Tinbergen undermines a sharp metaphysical contempt for animals. Animals are not mere automata; they are organized, responsive, adaptive beings with species-specific worlds. This does not decide whether animals have souls in any particular theological tradition, but it makes morally and theologically crude denials of animal interiority less plausible.

13.4 Against reducing meaning to survival

The theological caution is equally important. If Tinbergen is read as saying that the meaning of every action is simply its survival value, theology must resist. A human act can have biological function and also moral, symbolic, sacramental, or spiritual meaning. The Dutch critique of Tinbergen is valuable precisely because it asks whether human gestures have “lived-experience” values beyond biological utility.

13.5 Religion as behaviour, but not only behaviour

Tinbergen’s method can study religious behaviour: ritual, posture, chant, fasting, pilgrimage, prayer gestures, group synchrony, moral formation, fear, consolation, awe, and attachment. It can ask causal, developmental, functional, and evolutionary questions. But it cannot, by itself, decide whether God exists, whether prayer is answered, whether grace is real, or whether worship is true. Those are theological and metaphysical questions, not ethological ones.

A Tinbergian theology should therefore welcome biological explanation while refusing biological imperialism.

14. Main weaknesses and dangers in a Tinbergian philosophy of mind

A rigorous extraction must include the problems.

First, Tinbergen’s anti-subjectivism can become too restrictive. If one is not careful, methodological caution about subjective states becomes an inability to theorize consciousness. Later animal consciousness research had to reopen questions that classical ethology often bracketed.

Second, function can be mistaken for cause. Tinbergen himself warns against conceptual confusion, and later interpreters stress that one must not confuse levels of explanation. Saying that a behaviour has a function does not mean the animal performs it because it consciously aims at that function.

Third, adaptationist explanation can overreach. Not every trait is optimally adapted; not every human problem is best explained as mismatch; not every social norm is a disguised survival strategy. Tinbergen’s own autism work shows the danger of moving too quickly from observation to sweeping human diagnosis.

Fourth, ethological observation can be ethically dangerous when applied to vulnerable humans. Tinbergen’s autism theory is a warning case: close observation and humane intention do not guarantee correct etiology.

Fifth, Tinbergen lacks a full phenomenology. He gives us tools for studying embodied action, but not a rich first-person account of grief, hope, shame, prayer, deliberation, or selfhood.

Sixth, Tinbergen lacks a theory of normativity. Ethology can explain how moral behaviour may develop or function, but it does not by itself establish what is good, just, holy, sinful, or obligatory.

15. Final reconstruction: the Tinbergian philosophy of mind

A comprehensive Tinbergian philosophy of mind can be stated as follows.

The mind is the organized, embodied, developmentally formed, evolutionarily inherited, environmentally situated activity of a living organism. It is not reducible to externally visible motion, because behaviour depends on internal organization, bodily condition, feedback, affective state, developmental history, and nervous system function. But it is not accessible first as a private inner object either. It is studied through living action.

Consciousness is a special and difficult dimension of minded life. Tinbergen’s method does not solve the hard problem, but it tells us how not to cheat: do not explain behaviour by merely naming a subjective state; do not deny subjectivity merely because it is difficult; do not infer consciousness by sentimental projection; do not infer its absence from silence. Study the organism across mechanism, ontogeny, function, and phylogeny.

Tinbergen’s work is hostile to using occult terms as substitutes for biological explanation. Its real strength is elsewhere: it gives a disciplined way to study perception, action, affect, development, and world-involvement without reducing minded life to a single level of analysis. For human beings, that framework still leaves room for language, moral responsibility, self-interpretation, and vulnerability, but it does not pretend to settle more than Tinbergen himself argued.

The result is a philosophy of mind that is biological without being flatly reductionist, embodied without being merely mechanical, and comparative without erasing human distinctiveness.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
